Portfolio UX Case Study · 2023
Case Study

UX Tree
Mentorship

Redesigning a mentorship platform's navigation and mentor discovery experience — so early-career UX designers can find the guidance they need with confidence.

UX Tree

2023

UX ResearcherUX / UI Designer

Figma · Maze · Miro · Google Docs

UX Tree connects early-stage UX designers with experienced industry professionals. This project tackled two core experience gaps: users couldn't navigate the site confidently, and mentor information wasn't compelling enough to drive applications.

Focus Area 01

Homepage Navigation & Clarity

Users struggled to understand what the platform offered and where to go next. The navigation lacked hierarchy and the homepage messaging was ambiguous — particularly for early-stage designers.

Focus Area 02

Mentor Discovery & Profile Depth

The Mentors page failed to surface the details that drive decisions — career backgrounds, skills, mentoring approach, and social proof. Users left without enough information to feel confident applying.

Project Details

Defining the Problem

Problem Identification

User research revealed a consistent pattern: visitors arrived with genuine interest but left confused. The navigation was unclear, mentor profiles lacked meaningful detail, and it was difficult to understand what joining the program would actually involve.

Defining Scope

Interviews and usability sessions made the scope clear: users needed more context about mentor backgrounds, a simpler application journey, and a page structure with stronger visual hierarchy. Button contrast, layout logic, and information architecture all required attention.

Methodology

Design Thinking Framework

I applied the Design Thinking framework throughout — moving from empathy and research through ideation and prototyping to validated solutions. Each iteration was informed by real user feedback.

Reference: nngroup.com — Design Thinking

The Key Problems

What wasn't working — and how to fix it

Problem 01

Mentor profiles didn't build trust or drive action

The "Our Mentors" page presented mentors with insufficient detail. Career history, skills, and personality were largely absent, leaving users unsure who to connect with — or even how to view a full profile.

Design Improvements
  • Surface key credentials on mentor cards for at-a-glance evaluation
  • Redesign the profile structure for clarity and visual hierarchy
  • Present information in a more engaging, scannable format
  • Make the path to a full profile obvious and inviting
Problem 02

The program itself was hard to understand

Users found it difficult to grasp how the mentorship program worked, what the benefits were, what would be expected of them, and whether they could choose their mentor.

Design Improvements
  • Restructure navigation labels for clarity and intent
  • Create a dedicated page explaining how the program works
  • Clearly communicate expectations and commitments for mentees
  • Explain the mentor-matching process transparently
Research

Understanding users before designing for them

I conducted user research to uncover the real friction points — through competitive analysis, user surveys, and moderated usability sessions. This gave me a clear picture of what users expected and what would give them confidence to apply.

Competitive Analysis

I analysed three direct competitors — MentorCruise, My UX Academy, and ADP List — to identify design patterns that build user trust and support mentor discovery.

🧭 MentorCruise

Clear mentor/mentee distinction, visible CTAs, and detailed profiles with mentee reviews create a high-trust environment.

🎓 My UX Academy

Specialised UX focus with a practical, portfolio-driven curriculum. Blended online and in-person offerings signal credibility.

📋 ADP List

Visually rich mentor listings filtered by skill, with prominent mentee reviews for fast evaluation.

💡
Key Insight

Users need credible mentor information and visible social proof before they'll commit to applying. Platforms that surface this clearly see significantly stronger conversion from visitor to applicant.

Survey Findings

The majority of respondents had not previously joined a mentorship program — not due to lack of interest, but limited awareness. Respondents prioritised mentors with strong industry experience, senior-level expertise, and clear communication skills.

Usability Study — Top 3 Pain Points

I ran a moderated usability study covering three key tasks. Sessions surfaced recurring confusion around navigation labels and homepage messaging — users weren't sure where to click or what the platform was for.

Personas

Insights were synthesised using affinity diagrams and empathy maps. Two personas emerged, representing the platform's primary user groups.

Problem Statement

Both personas pointed to the same core gap on the Mentor page: not enough detail to make an informed, confident decision.

Problem Statement — James

James, a UX designer transitioning from a computer science background, needs a mentor with proven expertise in user research and usability testing because he requires structured, personalised guidance to build the practical skills and confidence needed to fully transition into UX design.

Ideate

From insights to design decisions

With a clear problem statement and two grounded personas, I moved into ideation using Crazy 8 paper prototyping to rapidly explore layout directions for the Mentor page and profile view.

Prototype 1 — User Flow

View Prototype 1 in Figma

Mentor Page — Before & After

Original
ux-tree.com/mentors — Original
Redesigned
ux-tree.com/mentors — Redesigned

Changes to the Mentor Page

  • Page Title Added "Our Mentors" heading with a supporting hero image — reduced in size to prioritise content
  • Navigation Retained the original menu structure, adding dropdown indicators to subcategory items
  • "Full Profile" Button A clear CTA on each mentor card signals that more information is available
  • Core Mentor Info Name, current job title, and tenure with the program displayed prominently on each card
  • Skills Highlight Each mentor's strongest areas surfaced at card level for fast scanning

Mentor Bio Page — Before & After

Original
ux-tree.com/mentor/krystal — Original
Redesigned
ux-tree.com/mentor/aisling — Redesigned

Changes to the Mentor Bio Page

  • Header Mentor photo, name, and job title presented prominently at the top
  • About Me A personal introduction covering professional background, interests, and approach
  • Skills A clearly structured list of the mentor's key skills and areas of expertise
  • Case Studies Selected examples of past mentoring work, adding credibility and context
  • Experience & Education A clear career and academic history to support informed decision-making
  • Mentee Reviews Social proof from previous mentees to build trust and transparency
Testing

Prototype 1 — Testing & Findings

An unmoderated usability test was run in Maze with six early-stage UX designers. Participants navigated to a mentor's full profile and provided qualitative feedback on layout and content. All three tasks were completed — but feedback surfaced clear areas for iteration.

33%

of users spent too long on individual screens

42%

average misclick rate across tasks

6/6

participants completed all assigned tasks

  • The "About" section was too brief and lacked personality or depth
  • No information on how the mentor originally entered the UX field
  • Mentor portfolios and case studies were absent entirely
  • Inconsistent font usage created a visually noisy experience
  • Missing borders, colour accents, and visual structure made sections feel undefined
Iteration

Prototype 2 — What changed

  • Mentor name and photo are now clickable and link directly to the full profile
  • The "About Me" section was significantly expanded, including how the mentor entered the UX field
  • Portfolio work and case study examples were added to each mentor profile
  • Skills and topic areas are now displayed more prominently with improved visual treatment
View Prototype 2 in Figma
Final Design

Live Prototype

ux-tree.com — Redesigned Experience
→
Redesigned
Additional Findings

Homepage messaging was excluding the target audience

The homepage was inadvertently communicating that UX Tree was for experienced designers. Renaming the hero headline to "Build your UX career" and restructuring the page immediately improved comprehension during testing.

  • Simplified homepage with a single, focused message for early-career designers
  • Key program details moved to a dedicated "About the Mentorship" page
  • Navigation labels updated to match how users actually think and search
Original Homepage
ux-tree.com — "Grow your UX career"
Redesigned Homepage
ux-tree.com — "Build your UX career"
Reflection

What I learned

🎯

Start with empathy, not assumptions

Sitting with users early in the process saved significant rework and led to solutions grounded in real behaviour.

🗂️

Structure is a UX decision

Information architecture choices directly affected whether users felt confident or confused. How content is organised matters as much as how it looks.

🎨

Visual design earns trust

Improved mentor profiles with consistent hierarchy, skill tags, and reviews increased perceived credibility and user confidence.

💬

Clarity converts

Transparent messaging about program expectations and mentor matching removed friction and drove action.

🔁

Iterate with intention

Each round of testing revealed something the previous iteration missed. Feedback is only valuable when applied with discipline.

What's Next

Next Steps

01

Further Usability Testing

Validate the latest design iteration with a fresh cohort of early-stage UX designers to confirm improvements and surface any remaining friction.

02

Mobile-First Optimisation

Audit and refine the experience across all screen sizes, ensuring navigation, mentor cards, and application flows work seamlessly on mobile.

03

Behavioural Analytics

Implement event tracking to monitor navigation patterns, identify drop-off points, and prioritise future improvements with data.

04

Continuous Feedback Loop

Establish an ongoing mechanism for collecting mentee and mentor feedback post-launch to adapt as the community grows.

05

Content Expansion

Develop additional resources — FAQs, onboarding guides, and program explainers — to support mentees at every stage of their journey.
